title: Edge Detection Framework Utilizing SOT-MTJ Bit-Cell Arrays
abstract: Traditional edge detection algorithms, foundational to computer vision, face significant challenges in energy efficiency and processing latency on conventional CMOS-based hardware. Existing algorithms, such as Canny, are computationally expensive, posing challenges in resource-constrained hardware where energy efficiency and low latency are critical. This study introduces a novel, hardware-efficient algorithm that leverages the intrinsic characteristics of magnetic tunnel junction (MTJ) devices. We present a detailed device-level analysis of an MTJ-based system for edge detection, outlining its operational cycles, including write, read, and reset methods. The algorithm's efficacy is evaluated against the standard Canny edge detection method. We provide a quantitative performance analysis, including metrics such as energy consumption and latency, which demonstrates that our proposed spintronics-based approach offers a promising solution for achieving low-power, high-speed image processing.
summary: This paper proposes an energy-efficient hardware accelerator for edge detection by leveraging the switching properties of SOT-MTJ magnetic tunnel junctions. By mapping the convolution operation onto the read/write cycles of these spintronic cells, it achieves low latency and significantly reduced power consumption compared to conventional CMOS-based algorithms. This work demonstrates a viable path for integrating advanced memory devices into real-time image processing hardware.
main_problem: Traditional edge detection algorithms are too computationally expensive and energy-intensive for resource-constrained hardware.
main_result: The proposed spintronics-based approach using SOT-MTJ arrays achieves low-power, high-speed image processing, significantly outperforming standard methods like Canny detection.
method: The method involves converting color images to grayscale, splitting pixel values into bit channels, and then performing convolution using the switching characteristics of SOT-MTJ cells.
model_or_system: The system utilizes Magnetic Tunnel Junction (MTJ) devices integrated into an SOT-MTJ bit-cell array for in-memory computing. The physics is governed by the Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert-Slonczewski (LLGS) equation, using Spin-Orbit Torque (SOT) for switching.
key_observables: Energy consumption (e.g., $1.5 	ext{ fJ}$ read), latency ($\sim 6 	ext{ ns}$ cycle time), and the resulting binary edge map derived from read current thresholds.
important_parameters: TMR (150%), $ho_{HM}$ ($100 	ext{ } \mu\Omega	ext{-cm}$), and the specific current levels corresponding to '1' (high current) and '0' (low current) inputs.
main_assumptions: The read operation is designed to avoid read disturbance by applying a small voltage ($\sim 0.1 	ext{ V}$), and the analysis relies on specific material parameters for the MTJ structure.
figures_summary: Figure 1 details the SOT-MTJ schematic and bit-cell operation. Figure 2 shows the image preprocessing pipeline (RGB to Grayscale to Bit Channels). Figure 3 illustrates the $3 	imes 3$ kernel convolution readout. Figure 4 compares the SOT-MTJ output against the conventional Canny output for test images.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the limitations of classical edge detection. It then details the physical model of the SOT-MTJ device, including its switching physics and operational cycles. The core contribution is the implementation of the edge detection algorithm using these devices, followed by a quantitative performance comparison against established methods.
why_interesting: While the core physics is spintronics/solid-state, the integration of device physics (LLGS, SOT) to solve a classical signal processing problem (edge detection) is highly relevant to emerging memory computing paradigms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: (a) Schematic of the SOT-MTJ device, on top of the heavy-metal (HM) layer. (b) Schematic of the bit-cell of SOT- MTJ with the access transistors for the write, read, and reset mechanisms. (c) Variation of different signals across the bit- cell and the mz with time.

Fig. 2: (a) Preprocessing: Original image split to RGB channels, and converted to a single-channel grayscale image. A sample of 2×2 pixels demonstrating binary conversion and bit-wise channel split. (b) Splitting of the grayscale images according to unsigned 8-bit integer channels.

Fig. 4: Comparison between the proposed SOT-MTJ-based edge detection method and the conventional Canny algorithm. (a) and (b) show the original test images used for evaluation. (c) and (d) present the corresponding edge maps generated by Canny operator, (e) and (f) demonstrate the edge detected image using our SOT-MTJ based approach.

Fig. 3: (a) A 3×3 kernel (red squares) is convolved over the MSB channel of the image. Each pixel of the kernel is implemented using a SOT-MTJ bit cell along with two access transistors, shown in enlarged scale. (b) Electrical readout from the kernel, illustrating the three possible cur- rent levels—9IP for all-1 input (no edge), 9IAP for all-0 input (no edge), and intermediate summed current values for mixed inputs (edge detected). (c) The resulting binary edge image produced after thresholding the read current response, highlighting detected object boundaries in the original image.
